guides.title: Frozen Pipes: What to Do
guides.summary: Quick steps to thaw a frozen pipe safely and prevent a burst.

sections.heading: Spot the warning signs
sections.body: If only a trickle comes out when you open a faucet on a cold day, a pipe is likely frozen. Pipes against exterior walls and in unheated spaces (basements, crawl spaces, attics) freeze first.

sections.heading: Shut off the water
sections.body: Locate your main water shut-off valve and know how to use it. If a pipe bursts while frozen, shutting the main off fast can save thousands of dollars in damage.

sections.heading: Thaw it gently
sections.body: Open the faucet so water can flow as the pipe thaws. Apply heat with a hair dryer, heating pad, or towels soaked in hot water. Never use an open flame, blowtorch, or kerosene heater.

sections.heading: Prevent it next winter
sections.body: Insulate exposed pipes, seal drafts near pipes, keep cabinet doors open during deep freezes, and let a faucet drip if temperatures fall below 20°F.

sections.heading: When to call us
sections.body: If you can't find the frozen section, the pipe is inside a wall, or you see bulging or cracking — call right away. We can locate the freeze, thaw it safely, and replace any damaged section.

guides.title: Clogged Drain Quick Fixes
guides.summary: Try these before reaching for harsh chemicals.

sections.heading: Hot water + dish soap
sections.body: For a slow kitchen drain, pour a few squirts of dish soap followed by a kettle of hot (not boiling) water. The soap helps break up grease.

sections.heading: Baking soda and vinegar
sections.body: Pour 1/2 cup baking soda down the drain, follow with 1/2 cup white vinegar, cover the drain, wait 15 minutes, then flush with hot water.

sections.heading: Plunge it
sections.body: Use a cup plunger for sinks and tubs. Make sure there's enough water in the basin to cover the plunger cup, and seal off any overflow opening with a wet rag.

sections.heading: Skip the chemical drain cleaners
sections.body: Liquid drain cleaners can damage older pipes, fixtures, and septic systems — and they can splash back when you finally have to open the line. They also make the job more dangerous for the plumber called in afterward.

sections.heading: When to call us
sections.body: Recurring clogs, multiple drains backing up at once, gurgling toilets, or sewer smell are signs of a deeper blockage. We can snake or hydro-jet the line and inspect with a camera.

guides.title: Water Heater Basics
guides.summary: How to keep your water heater running longer.

sections.heading: Know your unit
sections.body: Most homes use a tank-style heater (40 or 50 gallons) powered by gas or electricity. Tankless heaters heat on demand and last longer but cost more upfront.

sections.heading: Set the temperature
sections.body: 150°F is too hot — it scalds and wastes energy. The Department of Energy recommends 120°F for a balance of safety and efficiency.

sections.heading: Flush the tank yearly
sections.body: Sediment builds up at the bottom of the tank and reduces capacity and efficiency. Once a year, attach a hose to the drain valve and let several gallons run out until clear.

sections.heading: Test the T&P valve
sections.body: The temperature/pressure relief valve is a critical safety device. Lift the lever briefly once a year — water should rush out and stop when you release. If it doesn't, replace the valve.

sections.heading: When to call us
sections.body: Rust-colored water, popping or rumbling sounds, water pooling under the tank, or no hot water at all means it's time. Most tank heaters last 8–12 years.

guides.title: Find a Hidden Leak
guides.summary: A small leak can waste thousands of gallons a year.

sections.heading: Read your water meter
sections.body: Turn off every faucet and water-using appliance. Note the meter reading, wait two hours without using any water, and check again. Any change means a leak somewhere in the system.

sections.heading: Check the toilet
sections.body: Toilets are the #1 source of silent leaks. Add a few drops of food coloring to the tank and wait 15 minutes without flushing. If color appears in the bowl, the flapper is leaking.

sections.heading: Look for telltale signs
sections.body: Watch for water stains on ceilings, warped flooring, mildew smell, peeling paint near plumbing, and unexpectedly high water bills.

sections.heading: Outdoor leaks
sections.body: Soft spots in the yard, unusually green patches, or pooling water near the foundation can mean a service-line leak. These need professional attention quickly to prevent foundation damage.

sections.heading: When to call us
sections.body: If your meter test shows movement but you can't find the source, we use acoustic and thermal leak-detection equipment to pinpoint hidden leaks without tearing up walls or floors.

guides.title: When to Call a Pro
guides.summary: Knowing the difference can save your home and your wallet.

sections.heading: Call immediately
sections.body: Burst pipe, sewage backup, no water at all, water heater leaking from the tank itself, gas smell near a gas water heater, or any flooding. Shut off the main and call us — these get worse fast.

sections.heading: Call this week
sections.body: Persistent low water pressure, recurring clogs, water-heater age over 10 years, fixtures that drip after replacing washers, or any DIY repair you're not 100% sure about.

sections.heading: Worth a phone consult
sections.body: Planning a remodel, adding a bathroom, switching to tankless, or buying a home and want a plumbing inspection. We're happy to walk through options before you commit.

sections.heading: Things you can DIY
sections.body: Replacing a toilet flapper, swapping a showerhead, changing a faucet aerator, or unclogging a P-trap. If you're comfortable and the shutoff valves work — go for it.

sections.heading: Why it matters
sections.body: Plumbing problems compound. A small drip becomes a stain, then mold, then a structural issue. The cheapest plumber visit is the one that catches it early.
